So we have, it's an interesting model.
We are building internally a tool.
So we are building a platform.
It would ostensibly be a similar arrangement to a SaaS company, but we are not SaaSing it.
So we are servicing as software.
Service as software-ing it.
So we're building it internally.
Our customers are ourselves for the product.
You know, customers for the product are ourselves.
But then we are using it to service client output.
We are creating client work, and that client work is the money-making part of the operation.
So we have a few million dollars in run rate right now, about seven million dollars or so, with a bunch of money in the pipe.
And we're doing pretty well from a client perspective.
We're bringing new clients in, keeping them happy, and then trying to reduce or keep churn as low as possible.
All the normal stuff you do in any client services org, like an agency or any other client services, you're dealing with a lot of the similar things there or here that you would be anywhere else.
However, we have this additional layer.
We're building this product internally that's helping us service the clients.
And the customer for that is all of our MEs and directors and CMs, the delivery team under me, internally here, that helps build the content and create the content.
That platform is, at its base level, it's a collection of automation tools and LLM APIs.
And it allows us to create more sophisticated guardrails and pipelines to sort of massage and give rails to the very spongy, non-deterministic output, typical output of an LLM.

If you ask it to write content or create content around topic X, Y, or Z, as you know, you can get incredibly variable results, not only from query to query, but even within the same query, some components would be great if it can't give you, if it does not know or cannot find something, it will invent something and give it to you, you know, et cetera.
So our workflows are basically designed to create a semi-controlled or at least manipulatable environment to allow us to create and leverage amounts of content.
So if, for instance, we have a client that wants, I don't know.
10 pieces of content a week.
Typically, you would have between two and three writers generating that much content a week, unless you wanted incredibly low quality content, right?
Then you can say, okay, one person will pound out 10 pieces and just put any gibberish in a box and boom, that's what you get.
So what we are looking to do is to create a world where we feel that we can deliver the quality of two or three writers per week for a client with one writer.
But not really a writer.
And that's kind of why I was talking to you and kind of how I want to outline this role.
Because we have a model in which the writer is actually the workflow itself.
So we have a workflow that, and the way this workflow works, I'll give you like a quick, just to show you.

And I'll just kind of give you.

We'll take – we fork and train models.
But right now, a lot of our workflows just use open AI or entropic models.
So we're not trying to create a low-level model.
We're not trying to create – you know, originate a model.

We instead – we're focused on this idea that every time you have a business process, we're starting with content, but we will eventually get around to other business processes like sales, operations, you know, maybe even HR, like a lot of things.
Because anytime you create a business process, there's –
A lot of messy middle that happens that just either lives in a person's brain or lives in a document that's siloed and you're unable to access.
And one of the things that LLMs are actually good at is collating disparate amounts or disparate chunks of information into cohesive flows.
As long as you tell it, don't elaborate.
Please stick with this corpus that we've given you.

And so what this platform allows us to do is break down any process that we go through into its component parts as granularly as we feel like and then allowing us to then give it instructions on where the process is weak, where it is strong, what to lean into, what to lean away from, and then capture all of that so that it makes it better over time.
So the workflows that we're running today, for some of our clients, they produce content that is within the 90th percentile of acceptable to them.
And then the human in the loop is there to...
Step in at all of those junctures to get it that extra 10% so someone can feel this is quality.
The assessment part of the process where you're like, you look at a piece of content and you're saying, hey, I can articulate to you how this is not good.
You know, it's not that it is I can tell you, oh, this just isn't good.
Okay, how?
Right.
And as you know, these are different skill sets.
Like a writer may be able to write a piece of content well, but not be able to articulate to you exactly why it is good.
You know, or if they write a piece of content that is not so good, it sometimes can require an editor to step in and show them and articulate to them.
Hey, you're missing a logical underpinning to this argument.
You're making an argument.
You're stating it as fact.
Where's the logical underpinning to this to help support it, make it even readable and, you know, a piece of quality content.
It can be something like it could be something.

Broad is stylistic, like an editor comes in and is able to step back from the work, get a little distance from it, and help you see, hey, this is a really skeletal structure, you need to flesh it out here, maybe in terms of pacing, you can actually hurry this, or this isn't germane, you know, this part of the topic is related, but it's really tangential.
And if we take it out, we can improve the flow, and it keeps it on point.
All of these things, by the way, that I'm talking about are all things that LLMs do.
Writers do it, too, which is probably why LLMs do it, because they've been trained on a lot of writing, not all of which is high quality, right?

And so there's a lot of elements to the articles that we generate with workflows now that are relatively easily correctable, as long as you can articulate properly the problem and the solution, and then ingest that into our models, into our own personal.
Workflow models.
And then those models can be then applied to new workflows going forward.

yeah, our internal system is essentially a collection of workflows linked together in automation with automation tooling.

That it needs to then generate an article.

They've given you this.

You've got things like supporting quotes and links, all of that already populated.
This is, you know, ready to sort of edit as an editor, right?
Then we also give it an additional step here, this post-processing step right here.

And this post-processing step does a lot of, applies a lot of tweaks and polishing that can be related to client input, our own tweaks and polishing to get rid of LLM whiskers and, you know, common issues, tweaks.
We can give it negative reinforcement, never ever give me a headline with a colon in it, you know, things like that.
You know, and then it polishes out those rough edges.
And then what theoretically is coming out the other end of that is an article, right, that you would be able to edit, you know, tweet.

And then delivered to an ME that can then, they could show the client, the client's like, great, this looks awesome, so good, or they can give us feedback, and then we'll publish it, we'll end up publishing it, right?

The process for sort of like coming up with the polishing instructions is probably the most important bit, because it's sort of like a sum total of everything we know in both on two vectors, two major levels.

One, everything we know about the way that these models actually perform, like what output they're giving us, what they're delivering to us, what they're missing, what's right, what's wrong, you know, where they're falling over.
And then the second layer is what the client cares about, right?
Because like, that's, that's obviously very important as well.
It's not just how good can our models be at one thing or another.
It's also what is really important to the client or germane to the client, because that may not be a generally well-written article.
They really be what they're looking for.
They could be looking for something that is very specifically good about topic X or topic Y, or they really care about one thing and not so much the other.
So as an example, this is a series of refinement instructions, something that we would feed into the polishing step that was written for a recent client.
So we talk with the client.
We have a kickoff call.
We talk about their needs, their wants, their desires, their hopes, their dreams.
We gather information from them about what their company does, what they really care about, what their ICPs are, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
And then we'll produce some calibration articles for them, do some editing of those calibration articles.
I'm very hands-on in that step of the process to make sure that we are presenting something to the client that we think is properly representing what our eventual quality will be for them.
And then we create a set of instructions that is basically like a guideline for writing for that company.
So, and we'll do this for every client because everyone is different.
Each workflow needs a separate set of instructions to probably...
Really right for that client.
As an example, this client, Aimbit, wants some specific things.
They want to teach first.
Like this is the CEO's big thing.
wants to be educational content, you know, so that they're viewed as an authority in the space, which makes sense.
They want to avoid overt sales pitches.
You'd be surprised at how often this is or isn't the case, right?
Sometimes they don't want overt sales pitches and we're like, no problem.
We're going to put a nice, clean CTA at the bottom that's basically like sponsored by Aimbit.
You know, enjoy.
We're experts in this.
If you want more, come talk to us.
You know, that's their lead gen.
That's their possible, you know, click through to have somebody land on a, you know, sales call page or whatever.
But some people don't have any marketing at all.
And so they really want this content to function as marketing for them as well.
So we would want to then integrate them, you know, oh, and remember, Aimbit can solve your solution, you know, more naturally throughout the piece.
So that's just comes out of client calls and.
You know, kind of calibration with the client.
And then, you know, core value and core identity and value prop.
Some of the things are really specific, you hyper specific.
As an example, they want to use secretless access or secretless authentication, not secretless security, which is, in fact, a different thing.
It's like a different concept.
And an LLM will easily substitute secretless security for secretless access.
You know, it can very easily do that, you know, and it does.
Because it does not, it's not, it does not have any understanding of the specificity there.
And so we basically create all this for them.
So that they, it knows how to write for this.
So this document becomes like sort of master check to where the workflows don't wander and drift off into the wilderness.
They stay really close to what this client wants from, from the output.
And our workflows, our system allows us to input these things, feed these in to the workflows so that what we get out is a...
Really solid rough draft or post-rough draft, rough draft plus, that we can then tweak a little bit and then it's good to go.
And what we found is that this enables a pretty high leverage interaction between the writers and how much content they can produce.
I did a content audit of all of our clients this past week.
And I edited, I was able to edit 40 articles in about a day, which is pretty high leverage.
You know, I'm fast, but this was much faster than I typically am.

You know, I have a lot of experience, asterisk, et cetera, but it still enabled me to be a lot more productive than I would normally be because I was able to use the automation tools to run a lot of things in aggregate that I was like, I knew I wanted from it.
Okay, hey, I need, here are the sources that you're going to use for this.
I need three statistics pulled from these sources that support the theory.
In this article, right?
We've got workflows for that.
Or we can build them, you know, things like that.
So basically the job here is manage writers.
But those writers are a series of workflows instead of writers.
And then you're able to get the output from the writers, validate what's good, what's bad, and most importantly, articulate it.
Because if we can't articulate it and can't say exactly what the difference between good and this or this and good and this and bad is, then we can't improve the workflows.
And we can't tell it, hey, do better next time.
Here's the polishing instructions.
Let's integrate that into our workflows at a lower level and help them improve over time.

Some people, it's a religious thing.
I get it, you know, a theological thing.
But that's the world we live in, right?
Like, we're eyeballs deep in it.
And the reason I joined is because I do believe that we have the potential to build an armature here that allows people to kind of be much higher leverage about the content that they make.
And we eventually will expose this platform to the public.
We haven't done it yet.
We will at some point offer it to the public.

But, you know, I mean, at TC, I often over and over and over again, I came across these opportunities where we could have built things like this, but were unable to because we were just incredibly poorly resourced.
Or, you know, we just got sold in this new library and it has this whole new idea, et cetera.
But I literally have an RDP I wrote in 2015 for automation.

Getting a good portion of our funding article publication, because a good chunk, I would say if you break it down on a percentage basis, about 70 to 80 percent of any funding article was relatively formulaic content.

It was the other 20 to 25 percent that was really vital and interesting and that required the human to synthesize new information and to come up with a thesis about whether this company would or wouldn't be successful or what their take is on or how it fits into the larger ecosystem.
That's the fun bit.
That's the juicy bit, the bit that you get to enjoy after you chew on the gristle of putting seven investors all in the order of how much they actually contribute.
into the fund and putting in the past data of funding for that company, how much they funded, who they raised from, what round it is, what date that was raised, you know, and.
And linking back to all of the previous coverage that we had written about that company previous to this article, and it's like, that's repeatable, right?
That's programmable information.
And then you drop a nice little open field for the writer to be like, great, I just get to concentrate on the bit that's human synthesis.
You know, that's the expert in me, the taste in me saying, this is right, this is wrong, this feels good, this feels bad, you know?
And I think that's why I'm attracted to what we're building here is because I do feel that the future of writing is, like, augmented and higher leverage.
And to pretend otherwise, I think, is kind of doing everybody a disservice because you will be able to access, like, higher echelons of, like, ambition.
You know, because there were many times where we wanted to, say, dig into the Panama Papers, but I don't have 15 people to run data analysis on.
were were
A document dump like that in my newsroom to then just take all that time.
No, in fact, nobody does.
That's why a nonprofit did it.
And then offered that in partnership with the Times and with other publications.
But what if like a solo writer had deep research workflows that allow them to pull those things apart, to run theses against them, to deprove or disprove them, and to collect out bits of information?
And then, of course, have methods to verify and guard rails to help make sure that those are fact-checked and true, et cetera.
But that opens up a pretty cool greenfield opportunity for niches and solo writers.
And I just see the future of the media landscape.
It's never going to consolidate again.
Or if it does, it'll be in fits and starts.
The vast majority of it will be incredibly fragmented, which means that high leverage is the key.
So you have somebody running a solo newsletter or a solo business or one to three people that is...
We're blow for blow with a newsroom like the Times.
That's already happening.
It has really been happening for a decade in some arenas here and there.
But I think it's going to become more deeper across the media landscape.
So for me, on the content side, it's really cool.
We're building these high-level systems for people like that.
And then if we are able to successfully build this, we should be able to apply it to other parts of business ops where there's a lot of messy middle stuff that gets lost.
Like sales is a good example of that, the sales pipeline, a lot of stuff.
If it's not entered into a CRM, it gets stuck in somebody's brain or in somebody's personal notepad, and it never really gets fed in.
It never gets to really improve your process, et cetera.
And there are many other areas where that will come in handy.

Okay.

kind of it.

Or both from us in their brand style, we have tools to generate those internally.
They use a combination of image generation and then also layer combination tools.
There's a tool called Bissell, which basically allows us to take a bunch of assets from a client and it splits those into layers and then can remix and recombine them.
So it's not actually generating any images.
It's just layering things differently.
So you can add copy to it in the brand's approved font and an image that's germane to the article and it's all built off of their existing assets.
So it just remixes.
Basically, you'd like paying a junior designer to like, oh, hey, here's 10 elements.
Make five different versions of this with these 10 elements, right?
And I think that's kind of like the way that that works.
And then some others we are generating more custom graphics for.
And then we will also, we're dabbling very lightly in video and audio right now, but eventually we'll do it all.

Is it news, you know, press releases?
No press releases.
We don't do PR or comms.
Maybe we will at some point, but we currently don't.
The major work streams we have right now are programmatic SEO or editorial original content or organic content.
So that will take the form of, okay, let's say, you know, somebody is, we have a startup that is, for lack of a better term, a legal startup.
They offer the ability to automate registered agent violence in every state in the United States.
It's called discern.com.
And if you don't know anything about registered agents, you're not the only one either.
But the fact, I did know a little bit.
Not as deep as this, but you essentially must have a registered agent filing or be filed as a registered agent if you are operating as a business in the state.
And this is just a corporate law thing.
And so it turns out that there are really only a couple of really big conglomerates that do this and everybody hates them.
So this startup is trying to like change that, you know, but good for them.
I hope they succeed because we will continue with them as a customer.
But for them, we needed to create, as an example, 50 different pages that focus on each state, the process of being a registered agent for each state, right?
Then we have clusters of content we can create around each of those 50 states.
How do we become a registered agent?
What's the filing dates of this registered agent?
Any search you could imagine that somebody would be doing around a registered agent in state X, Y, or Z, we need to generate.
Well, our workflows can program.
You have a human, double-checking, make sure, running fact-checking, looking at all that.
But we can generate easily 5,800 to 1,000 pages at a time and run checks on those to make sure that they're cool and then publish them on a regular schedule for the client.
That's programmatic.
We basically create a templated page.
There's only a handful of variables changing per page.
This is very much doable with most automation workflows.
wouldn't be too surprising for anybody to feel like, oh, yeah, I get how you could do that.

This would be much more like what you would consider to be blog posts or posts on their site, which is information that is related to the content or related to the topics around their area of business or expertise that they are hoping to use to cement their authority, to grow top-of-funnel traffic.
Just to make sure people are aware of them in general and view them as an authority or source of information on topic X, Y, or Z.
We have a handful of permutations of that.

know, like, yeah, Ramp is synonymous with that brand and with the media brand because they're essentially their sole and only an anchor sponsor.

It's not like, oh, by the way, you know, link to us, you know, every five words or whatever.
It doesn't need to be all that.
